cards.text: To declare an r-value reference, use {{c1::&&}} instead of {{c2::&}}. For example: {{c3::int&& rref = 5;}}

cards.front: What types of values can l-value references and r-value references bind to?
cards.back:
L-value references (int&) bind to l-values (modifiable objects).
R-value references (int&&) bind to r-values (temporaries, literals, and values about to be destroyed).

cards.question: Which lines compile and which don't?
cards.code:
int x = 5;
int& lref1 = x;      // line 1
int& lref2 = 5;      // line 2
int&& rref1 = x;     // line 3
int&& rref2 = 5;     // line 4
cards.answer: Lines 1 and 4 compile. Lines 2 and 3 don't compile.
cards.explanation: L-value references can't bind to r-values (line 2). R-value references can't bind to l-values (line 3). Line 1 binds l-value ref to l-value. Line 4 binds r-value ref to r-value.

cards.text: A {{c1::const}} l-value reference can bind to both l-values and r-values, which is why it's commonly used for {{c2::function parameters}}.

cards.front: What is the primary purpose of r-value references in C++11?
cards.back: To enable move semantics and perfect forwarding. R-value references allow functions to differentiate between l-values (which should be copied) and r-values (which can be moved).

cards.text: An r-value reference {{c1::variable}} is actually an {{c2::l-value}}, because it has a name and an identifiable memory address.

cards.question: Does this code compile?
cards.explanation: Even though x is declared as an r-value reference, the variable x itself is an l-value (it has a name). func expects an r-value reference, so you'd need func(std::move(x)).

cards.concept: Function overloading with l-value and r-value references
cards.explanation:
You can overload functions to have different behavior for l-values vs r-values:
  • void func(const T&) - called for l-values (copy)
  • void func(T&&) - called for r-values (move)
This enables move semantics.

cards.text: When a function returns by value, the returned value is an {{c1::r-value}}, which enables {{c2::move semantics}} instead of copying.

cards.front: When should you use r-value references as function parameters?
cards.back: When implementing move constructors, move assignment operators, or functions that need to take ownership of temporary objects. Don't use them for regular function parameters unless you specifically need move semantics.

cards.text: Binding an r-value to an r-value reference {{c1::extends}} the lifetime of the temporary until the reference goes {{c2::out of scope}}.
